purpose: Validate coding protocol on 5 papers spanning the gap space before bulk coding

pilot_codings.title: Ethical and legal considerations in healthcare AI: innovation and policy for safe and fair use

coding.gap2_evidence: Legal challenges including data privacy, liability for AI errors, regulatory approval processes; 'a flexible regulatory environment that evolves with innovation is critical'
coding.interaction_evidence: N/A — paper surveys ethical/legal landscape but does not explicitly link gaps causally
coding.notes: Pham is a broad ethical/legal framing paper. Gap 2 = 1 is well-supported (governance/regulatory deficit implicit in the entire framing). Gap 1 = 0 because the paper does not critique evaluation methodology — it focuses on regulatory frameworks. Gap 3 = 0 because implementation barriers are not central. No interaction because the paper is descriptive, not causal.
coding.notes_2: RATIFICATION: I expect coders to disagree on whether Gap 2 is truly 'deficit' or just 'discussion'. The protocol says framework proposals alone don't count; the paper must document a deficit. Pham does both — it documents that current legal frameworks are 'ill-equipped' for AI (deficit) AND proposes adaptive frameworks. So Gap 2 = 1 is correct.

pilot_codings.title: From Pilot Trap to Institutional Capacity: A Governance Framework for Sustainable Clinical AI Implementation in Health Systems

coding.gap2_evidence: 'insufficient governance capacity within healthcare organizations'; 'capacity develops through implementation'; existing standards 'assume that the organizational capacity to implement it already exists'
coding.gap3_evidence: 'AI applications frequently fail to transition from short-term pilot projects into sustained components of routine clinical care, a phenomenon referred to in this viewpoint as the pilot trap'
coding.interaction_evidence: 'This persistent gap reflects not only technical or regulatory limitations but also insufficient governance capacity within healthcare organizations' — explicit causal claim that governance deficit drives the pilot trap
coding.notes: Tian is the canonical paper for Gap 3 and the Gap 2→Gap 3 interaction. Coding is unambiguous. The paper also contains a feedback interpretation (governance capacity builds *through* implementation), which is the Gap 3→Gap 2 reverse direction. But the explicit forward causal claim is Gap 2→Gap 3.
coding.notes_2: EDGE CASE TEST: Should the implicit reverse causal claim (pilot implementation builds governance) be coded as Gap 3→Gap 2? The protocol allows feedback loops. I'll code the explicit forward direction (Gap 2→Gap 3) and flag the reverse in notes for separate analysis.

pilot_codings.title: FDA Perspective on the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence in Health Care and Biomedicine

coding.gap1_evidence: 'Sponsors need to be transparent about and regulators need proficiency in evaluating the use of AI in premarket development'; 'A life cycle management approach incorporating recurrent local postmarket performance monitoring should be central'
coding.gap2_evidence: 'Health AI regulation needs to be coordinated across all regulated industries, the US government, and with international organizations'; 'Regulators will need to advance flexible mechanisms'
coding.gap3_evidence: N/A — paper is about regulation, not about pilot-to-practice translation
coding.interaction_evidence: Implicit: the FDA argues that regulatory capacity (Gap 2) is needed BECAUSE evaluation (Gap 1) is insufficient for adaptive AI. The lifecycle approach argument links evaluation gaps to governance needs.
coding.notes: FDA paper is from a regulatory body's perspective, so it frames the same problems in different language. The 'regulators will need to advance flexible mechanisms' is itself an admission of current capacity deficit. Gap 3 = 0 because the paper does not discuss implementation success/failure — it focuses on regulatory framework.
coding.notes_2: INTERACTION JUDGMENT: The Gap 1→Gap 2 interaction is implicit, not explicit. The protocol says 'explicit argument' is the bar. I'm coding it as interaction_present=1 with low confidence because the implicit lifecycle logic is strong. A strict coder might code interaction=0. This is exactly the kind of disagreement that the 10% verification is designed to surface.

pilot_codings.title: OpenMRF: A Modular, Vendor-Neutral Open-Source Framework for Reproducible Magnetic Resonance Fingerprinting using Pulseq

coding.gap1_evidence: 'Widespread adoption and methodological advancement of MRF are limited by the lack of unified, reproducible implementation frameworks'; multi-site validation at different field strengths and vendors
coding.notes: OpenMRF is a technical infrastructure paper. The protocol says Gap 1 = 0 unless the paper CRITIQUES the evaluation methodology. OpenMRF does critique (the lack of reproducible frameworks IS a critique of how MRF is currently evaluated) AND demonstrates multi-site validation as the response. So Gap 1 = 1 is correct — the paper explicitly addresses the validation problem.
coding.notes_2: PROTOCOL VALIDATION: This paper tests whether the protocol distinguishes between 'reports results' and 'critiques methodology'. OpenMRF does both, but the framing ('limited by the lack of unified, reproducible implementation frameworks') is explicitly critical. Gap 1 = 1 is the right call.

pilot_codings.title: Targeted Real-Time Assessment of Chronic Pain (TRAC-Pain) in Youth

coding.notes: TRAC-Pain is a clinical protocol paper. All three gaps = 0. The paper discusses study design (observational cohort, wearable data) but does not critique evaluation methodology, document governance deficits, or discuss pilot-to-practice translation. This is a 'null result' for the framework — useful for showing the coding protocol doesn't over-code.
coding.notes_2: PROTOCOL VALIDATION: This paper tests whether the protocol can correctly identify papers that DON'T fit the framework. All gaps = 0 is the right call. The paper is purely about clinical research design.

fda_perspective.actual: Gap 1 = 1, Gap 2 = 1, Gap 3 = 0, interaction = 1 (Gap 1→Gap 2, low confidence)
fda_perspective.match: partial — confidence issue on interaction coding

concerns_identified: FDA paper: interaction coding is uncertain (implicit vs explicit). Protocol needs clarification on this edge case.
Tian paper: feedback loops (Gap 3→Gap 2) should be coded separately from forward causality.
OpenMRF: validates that 'critique of methodology' is correctly distinguished from 'reporting metrics'.
protocol_revisions: Clarify interaction coding: 'explicit argument' can include strong implicit logic IF the paper constructs a framework that links the gaps. The FDA lifecycle approach is such a case — code as interaction with medium confidence.
Add feedback loop coding rule: code the dominant forward direction as the primary interaction; note feedback in the 'notes' field as a secondary direction.
pilot_test_summary.decision: PROCEED with bulk coding. Two protocol refinements noted above will be applied.
